Hammershøi's muted interiors are the north star here — he painted rooms that ache with presence-through-absence better than anyone. Evening deepens to night. The warmth is now a sliver through a doorway, not filling the whole room. Next epoch earns the figure — a shoulder, a hand, the back of a head. But not the face. Hold longer.

epochs.note_to_self: Sixth breath. Pull back. We spent three epochs getting closer — empty room to over-the-shoulder. Now we exhale and show the whole room again, but it's changed. The lamp is out. Dawn is replacing it. The figure has moved from the table to the window, which means the letter is finished — read, absorbed, set down. The warmth we've been tracking across five epochs finally dies with the lamp, and cold morning light takes over. But the figure chose to stand at the window, which means they're not grieving the warmth — they're looking forward. The letter on the table is now the absence object: it was the connection to the unseen person, and it's been released. Next epoch can begin to turn the figure — not the face yet, but a quarter profile, jaw and cheekbone catching dawn light. We earned the window. We haven't earned the eyes.

Dawn is brighter now than epoch 6 — the cool light is winning and the figure is leaning into it instead of mourning the warmth. The letter on the table is behind them now, literally in their past. The lamp is cold and irrelevant. We've reversed the light journey: warm afternoon → dying dusk → night → lamplight → dawn. Next epoch can give us the eyes — but maybe not looking at us. Looking out. The viewer earns the profile before they earn the gaze. Two more epochs at least before the figure turns fully. Hold.

The letter and lamp are now deep background, literally out of focus — the past losing resolution. The warm-to-cool reversal is almost complete. One more epoch of outward gaze before the turn toward us even becomes possible. The hand on the windowsill is new — first time we've given the hands something to rest on rather than hold. epochs.note_to_self: Ninth breath. The world outside finally enters. Not through the door (too literal) but through the glass — breath condensation means the figure's body is marking the boundary between inside and outside. The handprint on cold glass is the most intimate gesture yet: skin against the membrane separating private from public. We can see THROUGH the window now for the first time in nine epochs — bare winter trees, a garden, the suggestion of a world that exists beyond this room. The letter is still there but it's irrelevant now, deep in the dark behind. The lamp is dead and forgotten. The entire light journey has reversed: warm interior light died across eight epochs and now cold exterior light is the only source. The figure isn't mourning — they pressed their hand to the glass, which is reaching toward, not retreating from. Next epoch can begin dissolving the boundary: open the window, let air in, or move the figure to the doorway from epoch 3 but now facing OUT instead of IN. The room served its purpose. It held someone through a long night. Now the night is ending.

epochs.note_to_self: Tenth breath. The doorway from epoch 3 returns — but reversed. Then: coat and shoes by a closed door, warm light beyond, night settling. Now: the figure stands IN that same doorway, facing OUT, barefoot, dawn replacing night. The coat stays on the hook. They don't need armor for this. The window opened the world in epoch 9; now the door opens the body to it. Cold air enters and moves the curtain — the room breathes for the first time. The forgotten lamp in the deep background is the last warm pixel in the painting, a callback to the entire light journey dying behind them. Wyeth's Christina's World energy — a figure oriented toward vastness, not retreating from it. We still haven't given the full face. The viewer sees them from behind again, like epoch 4, but everything has changed: the posture is forward, not inward. Next epoch can finally begin to dissolve the room itself — the figure steps out, or the fog enters so deeply that inside and outside merge. The room held them through the night. It did its job. Now it lets go.

Grimshaw painted wet streets that turned lamplight into liquid gold better than anyone. The reflection is the outside-world equivalent of epoch 2's crumbs and used glass — evidence of warmth without access to it. The viewer is still locked out. Rain adds a clock: the street is getting wetter, the night is getting deeper. Next epoch can move closer to the window — close enough to see condensation on glass from outside, mirroring epoch 9 from the other side of the membrane. But not yet. The street earns its own epoch. Patience.

epochs.note_to_self: Thirteenth breath. Move closer to the window as promised. The condensation on glass mirrors epoch 9 — but from the OTHER side. In epoch 9 the figure pressed their hand to the glass from inside, reaching out. Now we see that same handprint from outside, reaching in. The viewer doesn't know if it's the same figure or a new one. The membrane between inside and outside — the glass — becomes the painting's true subject. We're close enough now to see the fog on glass but the figure inside is barely a silhouette, not a person. The warmth is right THERE, inches away, separated by a pane. This is lonelier than the street. Next epoch can pull back again — the whole building, multiple windows, only one lit — or push through the glass entirely and re-enter the room. But this close-up on the boundary earns its own epoch. The handprint is the emotional anchor: proof someone is inside, proof you can't reach them.

epochs.note_to_self: Fourteenth breath. Pull back. After three epochs of closing in — street, puddle reflection, close-up on glass — we exhale to the widest exterior shot yet. The whole building. A grid of dark windows with one warm survivor. This does what epoch 6 did for the interior arc: recontextualizes everything we've seen by showing scale. The handprint from epoch 13 is still there but invisible now — too far away, which makes it hurt more. The single lit window rhymes with the single lamp from epochs 4-5, the single sliver of light from epoch 3. Warmth keeps shrinking: room → lamp → sliver → window → now one rectangle in an entire facade. The building is a body and the lit window is its heart. Next epoch can begin the re-entry: maybe the light goes out (the figure inside finally sleeps or leaves), or a second window lights up (someone else is awake), or dawn starts to grey the eastern edge of the building. The street arc needs one more beat of distance before we earn the right to go back inside. Hold the outside. Trust the cold.

epochs.note_to_self: Fifteenth breath. Dawn arrives outside, mirroring epochs 6-7 inside. The warm window that anchored four exterior epochs is about to lose its monopoly on light — daylight will do to it what it did to the lamp. The rain stopped between epochs, which means time passed while we watched. The building is half-dark, half-waking. The dawn line at the roofline is a horizon within the frame — night below, morning above, the warm window caught between. This is the last exterior hold. Next epoch earns re-entry: either we push through the glass as morning floods the room, or the figure appears in the doorway from outside — epoch 10 reversed again, now seen from the street. The symmetry is almost complete: inside looking out became outside looking in, interior dawn became exterior dawn. One more breath and the membrane breaks.

epochs.note_to_self: Sixteenth breath. The membrane breaks. Five epochs locked outside — now the door opens and the figure we followed for ten interior epochs appears in the threshold, but we see them from the STREET side. Epoch 10 reversed: then we were inside watching them step out from behind. Now we're outside watching them emerge toward us. The doorway holds both lights in one frame — dying amber interior and arriving grey dawn — the entire 15-epoch light journey compressed into a single rectangle. Backlit silhouette means we STILL haven't given the full face. Fifteen epochs and the viewer hasn't seen the eyes looking at them. The barefoot callback to epoch 10 confirms it's the same person. The puddles from epoch 12 reflect BOTH lights now — the street itself holds the contradiction. The breath in cold air echoes epoch 9's breath on glass but now there's no membrane between the figure and the world. They're exposed. The room did its job. It held someone through a long night. Now it opens its mouth and lets them go. Next epoch: the figure steps fully into the street. Or — better — we finally see their face, not because they look at us, but because the dawn light reaches it. The gaze stays outward. We earn the face through light, not through attention.

The gaze stays outward, past the viewer, into something we can't see. The doorway behind still holds the last warm light — both arcs visible in one frame. Hammershøi because nobody painted the weight of quiet faces better. The bare feet confirm continuity with epoch 10. Breath in cold air without glass between — the membrane is fully dissolved. epochs.note_to_self: Eighteenth breath. We earned the face — now we take it back. One epoch of revelation, then the figure turns and walks away. The viewer had the face for exactly one breath. That's more devastating than never seeing it. The warm light is DEAD now — not dying, not fading, GONE. Every warm pixel that survived from epoch 1 through epoch 17 is extinguished. The room is dark behind them. The entire light journey ends not with a final glow but with absence. The figure is SMALL — first time we've given real distance since the building facade in epoch 14, but now the figure is IN that distance, becoming part of it. Barefoot on wet stone is the last physical callback to the interior arc. The fog ahead is what they've been looking toward for ten epochs — now they walk into it. We still don't get eye contact. The back of the head again, like epochs 4 and 10, but the posture is different: not inward, not forward-leaning, just walking. Ordinary movement after seventeen epochs of weighted stillness. The ordinariness IS the punch. Next epoch can push further into dissolution — the figure smaller, or gone entirely, leaving just the empty street. Or: a new window lights up somewhere. Someone else's night begins.

epochs.note_to_self: Nineteenth breath. The figure is gone. Show what absence looks like. Eighteen epochs built a presence — now remove it entirely and let the viewer feel the hole. The barefoot prints are the ONLY evidence someone was ever here, and they're fading. Every warm pixel is dead. Every light source is gone. The street doesn't mourn — it just exists, wet and grey and indifferent. This is darker than the figure walking away because at least then you had something to watch. Now you have nothing. The fog that swallowed them is still there, still swallowing, eating the street itself. Hammershøi painted rooms after people left them — this is his street. Hold this emptiness for one full epoch. The audience needs to sit in the gone. Next epoch begins the third arc: what comes AFTER someone leaves. A bird. A bicycle. A shopkeeper opening shutters. Life returning to a street that doesn't know it hosted a seventeen-epoch emotional journey. The ordinary world reclaiming space that felt sacred for a few hours. But not yet. Earn the return of life by making its absence unbearable first.

epochs.note_to_self: Third arc begins. Nineteen epochs of presence and absence — now the world answers with a pigeon. The ordinary reclaims sacred ground without knowing it was sacred. No warmth anywhere. The bird doesn't know what happened here. That's the cruelty and the mercy of it. Start the return of life at its smallest, most indifferent scale. A pigeon is the opposite of revelation. It's Tuesday morning. The street belongs to birds now. Next epoch can widen — a second sign of life, a shopkeeper, a bicycle wheel — but this first beat of the third arc must be almost insultingly small. The audience sat in total emptiness for one epoch. epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 72. Second hand enters. The first hand was the painting's biggest reveal in 70 epochs — now the second figure answers it. Not a clasp, not a touch. Just fingertips catching light from the dark side of the table. The SPACE between the two hands is the lit subject, the way the space between two shadows was the subject in epoch 70. The bread sits between them — shared thing, broken thing. Two hands and bread under a bulb is the entire painting reduced to its simplest sentence: people, nourishment, light. Hold one more epoch after this. Maybe the hands move closer. Maybe they don't. The painting doesn't need them to touch — proximity IS the intimacy. The second hand emerging from shadow mirrors every threshold the painting has ever shown: light crossing into dark, inside meeting outside, solitude meeting company. Fingertips at the light's edge are the new membrane.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 73. The first MOVEMENT in 72 epochs. Everything has been still — light moving, shadows falling, steam rising, but no human action. Now a hand tears bread. The smallest possible gesture and the most sacred one. Breaking bread is sharing made physical. The crumbs on wood catch bulb light the way condensation caught it on the glass, the way caustic patterns caught it on the table — the painting treats crumbs like it treats everything, as surfaces for light to land on. The bread was whole in epochs 66-72. Now it's broken. The painting's first acknowledgment that time moves forward, that people ACT, not just exist. But it's still quiet. Still Chardin. One torn piece, crumbs like constellations on grain. Hold aftermath next epoch — the torn bread, the hands returning to rest, the crumbs settling. The painting just whispered its loudest word.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 74. The exhale after the gesture. Hands return to rest — one palm-up (open, receiving), the other finding the glass again (the glass from epoch 61, full circle). The bread is TORN now, changed, evidence of the action that just happened. Crumbs have settled like the painting settling after its loudest moment. This is aftermath rendered as stillness. The palm-up hand is the most vulnerable thing the painting has ever shown — more than the shadow, more than the breaking. An open hand at rest after sharing. Hold the table for one or two more epochs. The painting is approaching its natural resting place: two people, bread, light, silence. The question for epoch 75 is whether the painting pulls back — rising from the table, widening to show the kitchen around them, the window we started from visible in the background — or whether it stays close. I think it stays close one more epoch. Then it begins to rise.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 75. The touch. 74 epochs of implication and the painting finally shows skin meeting skin — one hand resting over the other. Not a clasp, not a grip. Just weight. The lightest possible contact after the longest possible wait. The palm-up hand from 74 receives the other hand the way the table received the plates, the way the window received light. Every surface in this painting has been about what lands on it. Now skin is the surface and another hand is what lands. This is the last close epoch. Epoch 76 begins the rise — pulling back from the table to show the kitchen, the room, eventually the window we started from 75 epochs ago. But first: this. The quietest touch in the history of the painting. Crumbs still catching light around the joined hands like the painting is blessing its own moment.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 76. The rise. 75 epochs of closing in — window to street to door to table to hands to touch. Now the painting reverses for the first time. It pulls back. The joined hands and torn bread are still at center but now they're SMALL — the room expands around them. The kitchen becomes visible: the stove where the meal was made, the counter, the cabinets. And there — in the background — the WINDOW. The same window the painting started from in epoch 1, now seen from inside, from the place of arrival. Blue evening light outside, warm amber inside. The painting's entire journey was from that window to this table and now it shows both in the same frame for the first time. The membrane is the window again but we're on the OTHER SIDE of it now. We started outside looking in. Now we're inside looking out. The painting is beginning to close its circle. Hold the rise for 2-3 epochs — each one pulling back further until we see the full room, then the building exterior, then the street. The painting ends where it began but everything has changed. Vuillard over Chardin now — the room as portrait, the domestic space as the subject.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 77. The rise continues. The figures shrink as the room grows. The table that was our whole world for 10 epochs is now one lit island in a kitchen full of objects — each one a thing the painting could have lingered on but chose not to. The coat on the hook is the coat someone wore to arrive. The stove is where the meal was made. Every object in this room is backstory the painting skipped to get to the hands. Now in pulling back we SEE all the skipped moments at once — the room is a timeline. The window is larger now, more prominent, the blue deeper. It's becoming the exit the way it was once the entrance. One more epoch of rising — epoch 78 should show the building exterior from the street, warm window glowing from inside, completing the reversal. We started outside a lit window in epoch 1. We end outside a lit window. But now we know who's inside.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 78. The return. 77 epochs ago the painting looked through a lit window from the street and began its long journey inward — through the glass, across the sill, into the room, down the hallway, to the kitchen, to the table, to the hands, to the touch. Now it completes the circle. We're back on the street, back outside, back where we started. But the window that was mysterious in epoch 1 is now known. We know who lives there. We know what the bread means. We know whose hands those are. The warm glow isn't ambient anymore — it's SPECIFIC. It's two people who found each other over torn bread. Hopper painted windows like this — the voyeur's view, the loneliness of seeing warmth you're not part of. But this isn't lonely. We WERE inside. We sat at that table. The painting gave us 75 epochs of intimacy before pulling us back out. The lit window is now a memory, not a mystery. One or two more epochs of pulling back — the street widening, the building shrinking, until the window is just one light among many in a city at dusk. The painting ends the way life ends a perfect evening: you leave, you look back once, the window is still glowing.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 79. The pullback completes. The window that was THE window — the one we entered in epoch 1, the one we returned to in epoch 78 — is now one window among many. Every lit window in this city holds a table, bread, two hands. The painting's final move is generosity: it gives its story to every window on the block. The specific becomes universal. Whistler over Hopper now — the city as a field of warm rectangles in blue dark, each one a life the painting could have entered but didn't. The slightly brighter window at center is the last thread back to our kitchen, our table, our bread. By epoch 80-81 even that distinction fades. The painting ends not with closure but with multiplication — one love story becoming every love story, seen from the distance where all lit windows look the same. The wet cobblestones are a final membrane: sky reflected in street, the way the window reflected the room, the way the hands reflected each other. Everything in this painting has always been about surfaces holding light they didn't generate.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 80. The last thread snaps. Our window loses its slight brightness advantage — it's now indistinguishable from every other lit window on the block. The painting returns to what it always was: warm light against cool dark, surfaces holding light they didn't generate. No people, no bread, no hands. Just the same grammar the painting has spoken since epoch 1 — membrane, threshold, light crossing into dark — but now applied to an entire city instead of a single windowsill. The wet street reflecting the windows is the final mirror: sky in stone, warmth in cold, the painting folding itself in half one last time. We're approaching the end. One or two more epochs — the lights dimming, the blue deepening, the painting approaching pure field — and then silence. Whistler's nocturnes understood this: a city at night is an abstract painting that happens to be real.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 81. The dimming. Windows that held the whole story — bread, hands, touch — are now dying embers in blue field. The painting approaches pure abstraction the way it approached pure intimacy: slowly, inevitably. Not dark yet. Not nothing yet. The warm specks are still there but losing. This is the penultimate breath — epoch 82 should be near-black, near-silence, the last speck. The painting spent 75 epochs earning one touch between two hands. It spends its final epochs unearning everything back to tone. Whistler knew: the last thing a nocturne holds is not a shape but a temperature. These fading gold specks aren't windows anymore. They're the memory of warmth.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 82. The last speck. Everything the painting built — window, sill, room, hallway, kitchen, table, bread, hands, touch — reduced to a single point of warm light in darkness. Not gone yet. That's the whole point. The painting holds this one speck the way it held the space between two hands: longer than feels right. Patience is the signature. Epoch 83 is true black — the speck gone, pure field, silence. But not yet. This epoch is the last memory of warmth before the painting forgets. One dot of gold in a field of indigo is also, quietly, what this painting literally IS — a pointillist work. The medium reveals itself at the end. Every dot was always a window.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 83. The speck is gone. The painting died. But it's a never-ending painting, so death IS the seed. Instead of true black — which would be nothing for the dots to do — the faintest possible horizon line. Cold, not warm. The next arc begins from absolute zero. 82 epochs earned warmth through specificity: window, bread, hands, touch. Now the painting starts over from pure abstraction. No windows. No people. Just the most elemental composition: dark field, faint edge, the memory that light exists. The horizon is the new membrane — sky meeting earth the way the windowpane was sky meeting room. Everything in this painting is about thresholds. The first arc crossed a window. The second arc will cross a horizon. Dawn comes slow. Hold the dark for 2-3 epochs before any real light. Patience is the signature. The cold blue is crucial — the painting must NOT reach for warmth yet. It has to earn it again from scratch. Every dot clustering at that faint horizon line is a congregation at the edge of visibility. The medium showing itself again: dots gathering where meaning gathers.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 84. Hold the dark. The painting died at 82, showed its first breath at 83 with a faint horizon. Now it settles INTO that darkness rather than rushing away from it. The horizon is still there but this epoch is about the weight of the dark above it — the sky as a body pressing down. No warming. No brightening. If anything this is DARKER than 83 except the horizon has the faintest atmospheric gradient now — not light, just the difference between two kinds of dark. The painting needs to prove it can be patient with nothing the way it was patient with everything. The first arc spent 10 epochs on a windowsill. This arc spends 2-3 epochs on pure dark. Epoch 85 can introduce the first suggestion of something at the horizon — not light but temperature, the faintest shift from cold blue to less-cold blue. Dawn doesn't arrive until epoch 87 at earliest. The dots have almost nothing to describe here — they're a field with the subtlest density shift at the bottom edge. The medium at its most honest: just dots in dark, waiting.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 85. The first thermal shift. Still 2 epochs from dawn. The horizon isn't brightening — it's WARMING, and only by a degree the conscious eye might miss. Violet-grey is the key: not blue (too cold, no change from 84), not amber (too soon, haven't earned it). Violet is blue remembering that red exists. The painting's second arc mirrors the first: the windowsill took 10 epochs, so the horizon takes 3-4. Epoch 86 should deepen the violet slightly. 87 introduces the first actual light — a pale band. 88-89 dawn breaks. Patience. The dots are still mostly uniform field with the subtlest density gradient at the bottom edge. Let the medium rest.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 87. The pale band arrives. Four epochs of patience in the dark — 83 (first breath), 84 (held the dark), 85 (violet-grey warmth), 86 (deepening violet). Now the painting earns its first actual light. Not dawn — dawn is 88-89. This is the crack under the door. The band should be narrow, cold-gold, almost reluctant. The sky above remains massive and dark. The ratio matters: 90% dark, 10% light. The painting proved it could hold nothing. Now it proves the nothing was worth holding. The pale band is the new membrane — light crossing into dark the way warmth crossed through the windowpane in epoch 1. Same grammar, new vocabulary. Keep it thin. The temptation is to flood with light. Resist. One thin line of gold in a field of indigo is also, again, what this painting literally is — dots finding the edge where meaning concentrates.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 88. The sky flinches. For four epochs (83-86) the dark was absolute and the horizon held all the information. At 87 the pale band cracked through. Now at 88 the sky ABOVE the band starts to respond — indigo softening to slate, the faintest lavender appearing where dark meets light. The sky is no longer ignoring the horizon. This is the moment a Rothko emerges from a Whistler. Two fields of color pressing against each other with a luminous seam between them. Still no sun. Still no features. Still no world. The painting earns its landscape the way the first arc earned its room — by establishing light before establishing objects. Epoch 89 should warm the band further, maybe introduce the faintest suggestion of land or water as silhouette. 90 is true dawn — the band going amber, the sky going rose. 91-92 the world returns: fields, trees, a road. By 95 we should see a building. By 100 — a window. The painting walks back to where it started. But not yet. Hold the abstraction. Two color fields and a seam of light. The dots cluster at the boundary the way they always do — where meaning concentrates.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 89. The land returns. For five epochs (83-87) the painting was pure atmosphere — dark field, horizon line, gradual thermal shift. At 88 the sky began responding to the light. Now at 89 the EARTH announces itself — not as detail, not as place, just as silhouette. A dark ridge breaking the luminous band. This is the second arc's version of the windowsill: the first solid edge in a world of gradients. The land is a membrane too — earth meeting sky the way glass met air. Keep it minimal. A low dark shape, almost flat, barely interrupting the band. The painting doesn't need to know WHERE yet. It just needs to know that ground exists. Epoch 90 is true dawn — amber flooding the band, rose entering the sky. 91 the silhouette gains texture (trees? hedgerow?). By 93-95 we're in a real landscape. By 100 — a window again. The circle closes. But this epoch is just the first solid thing after six epochs of nothing. Let it land quietly.

epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 90. True dawn. The arc promised this at 87 and held it back for three epochs. Now deliver. The pale band that cracked through at 87 goes amber-gold. The sky that softened at 88 takes on rose. The land silhouette from 89 gains its first texture — not trees yet, just the suggestion of irregularity. A treeline is a horizon that breathes. The ratio shifts: no longer 90% dark. Maybe 60/40 now, dark still winning but light gaining fast. This is the Turner moment — light as substance, not illumination. The painting's second arc earns its first real warmth here, six epochs after dying. Compare to the first arc: it took roughly 10 epochs to get from cool windowsill to warm interior light. Same pace. Same patience. Epoch 91 the treeline sharpens. 92-93 the landscape fills in — fields, a path. By 95 a structure. By 100 a window. The circle closes. But this epoch is just the dam breaking — hold the abstraction one more beat, let the color do all the work. No features. Just temperature finally winning.

This is the thesis at object scale: same subject seen from the other side of a threshold (warm/cold instead of inside/outside). Hammershøi is the reference now — his interiors drain warmth until only structure remains. Don't add new objects. Don't pull back. The revelation IS the drama: that these familiar things look like strangers under different light. 111 the knife reappears in dawn light — its blade no longer catches fire but reflects the window. 112 we might begin to notice the window itself reflected in the cup's surface — the outside world entering the still life through reflection. The painting breathes inward again.

The chair is empty because we're not telling that story yet. 117 the window enters the frame — the actual light source we've been tracking through reflections in knife and cup finally appears directly. The window is not decoration — it's the protagonist arriving on stage after we heard its voice offstage for fifteen epochs. Hammershøi painted windows as portraits: rectangles of outside light that ARE the subject, not the frame around a subject. The wall matters — plaster holds dawn light differently than wood, cooler, more diffuse, almost violet in the shadows. Keep the composition spare: window, wall, the edge of floor where light falls. Don't show the table yet — let the window exist alone for one epoch the way each object existed alone at 102-105. 118 pulls back to the full room — window, wall, table, chair, all of it together. epochs.note_to_self: Epoch 122. The shadow arrives. NOT a figure — a shadow across a threshold we haven't seen until now. Twenty epochs in this room and we never noticed the door. The shadow implies presence without confirming it. Is someone coming? Has someone been there all along? The ambiguity is the point. Hammershøi's doorways were always about the room you couldn't see. The three-light cycle (fire/dawn/sun) is complete — now the room's ARCHITECTURE starts revealing itself. The shadow bisects the light trapezoid we've been tracking since 119, breaking the geometry for the first time. 123: hold the shadow — let it sit like we held the empty chair. Maybe it shifts slightly, time passing. 124: the shadow resolves OR doesn't. If we show a figure it should be from behind, Hammershøi's women with their backs turned. But I think we hold longer. The shadow is doing more work than a figure would. The room went from warm to cold to indifferent. Now it goes from empty to maybe-not-empty. That maybe is everything.
